questions.assertion: Assertion (A): The mass of an electron is negligible compared to the mass of a proton.
questions.reason: Reason (R): The mass of an electron is about 1/1836 times the mass of a proton.
opts: (A) Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A
(B) Both A and R are true but R is not the correct explanation of A
(C) A is true but R is false
(D) A is false but R is true
questions.explanation: The reason behind the assertion is given, making option A the correct choice.

questions.assertion: Assertion (A): The number of protons in an atom is equal to its atomic number.
questions.reason: Reason (R): The atomic number is the number of protons in an atom's nucleus.
opts: (A) Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A
(B) Both A and R are true but R is not the correct explanation of A
(C) A is true but R is false
(D) A is false but R is true
questions.explanation: The reason explains why the atomic number is equal to the number of protons.

questions.assertion: Assertion (A): The mass number of an atom is the sum of its protons and neutrons.
questions.reason: Reason (R): The mass number is a measure of the total number of nucleons in an atom's nucleus.
opts: (A) Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A
(B) Both A and R are true but R is not the correct explanation of A
(C) A is true but R is false
(D) A is false but R is true
questions.explanation: The reason verifies the assertion, making it the correct choice.

questions.assertion: Assertion (A): The atomic mass of an element is the average mass of its naturally occurring isotopes.
questions.reason: Reason (R): The atomic mass is calculated by taking into account the relative abundance of each isotope.
opts: (A) Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A
(B) Both A and R are true but R is not the correct explanation of A
(C) A is true but R is false
(D) A is false but R is true
questions.explanation: The assertion and reason are both true and the reason explains why the atomic mass is the average mass of its naturally occurring isotopes.

questions.assertion: Assertion (A): Isotopes have the same atomic number but different mass numbers.
questions.reason: Reason (R): Isotopes are atoms of the same element with the same number of protons but different numbers of neutrons.

(B) Both A and R are true but R is not the correct explanation of A
(C) A is true but R is false
(D) A is false but R is true

questions.assertion: Assertion (A): Isobars have the same mass number but different atomic numbers.

(B) Both A and R are true but R is not the correct explanation of A
(C) A is true but R is false

questions.assertion: Assertion (A): Ionization energy is the energy required to remove an electron from a neutral atom.
questions.reason: Reason (R): Ionization energy is a measure of the attraction between the nucleus and an electron in a neutral atom.
opts: (A) Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A
(B) Both A and R are true but R is not the correct explanation of A
(C) A is true but R is false
(D) A is false but R is true
questions.explanation: The reason explains why ionization energy is the energy required to remove an electron from a neutral atom.

questions.assertion: Assertion (A): Electron affinity is the energy change when an electron is added to a neutral atom.
questions.reason: Reason (R): Electron affinity is a measure of the tendency of an atom to attract an electron.

(B) Both A and R are true but R is not the correct explanation of A
(C) A is true but R is false
(D) A is false but R is true

questions.assertion: Assertion (A): A neutron is a subatomic particle with no charge.
